# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about agent-authored GitHub pull request outcomes, including dataset scale, merge-rate differences by agent and task type, quantitative associations between non-merged PRs and change size / CI failure, and a qualitative taxonomy of rejection patterns from manually annotated failed PRs.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: result
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study dataset contains 33,596 agent-authored pull requests across five coding agents.

## Source Excerpt

“Of the 33,596 agentic pull requests, the majority originate from OpenAI Codex... Copilot and Devin follow... while Cursor contributes 1,541, and Claude Code represents the smallest share with 459 PRs.”

## Note

This is the reported dataset size for the analyzed PR corpus.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Across all agents, 24,014 of 33,596 PRs were merged, for a reported merge rate of 71.48%.

## Source Excerpt

“Across all agents, 71.48% of PRs (24,014) are successfully merged.”

## Note

This is the aggregate merge outcome across the full dataset.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
OpenAI Codex accounts for 21,799 PRs, the largest PR volume among the five agents in the dataset.

## Source Excerpt

“Of the 33,596 agentic pull requests, the majority originate from OpenAI Codex (21,799), exceeding the output of any other agent by more than a factor of four.”

## Note

This evidence is about contribution volume, not merge quality by itself.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
OpenAI Codex has the highest reported merge rate in the dataset at 82.59% (18,004 merged PRs).

## Source Excerpt

“Despite handling the largest volume of contributions, OpenAI Codex achieves the highest merge rate, with 82.59% (18,004) of its PRs being accepted.”

## Note

This evidence is descriptive of observed outcomes by agent.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Copilot has the lowest reported merge rate among the five agents at 43.04% (2,139 merged PRs).

## Source Excerpt

“Copilot exhibits the lowest merge rate, with only 43.04% (2,139) of its PRs being merged.”

## Note

This evidence compares observed agent-level merge outcomes.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: result
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Documentation tasks have a reported overall merge rate of 84%, one of the highest task-type success rates across agents.

## Source Excerpt

“Across agents, tasks with consistently higher merge rates include documentations (84%), CI (79%), and build (74%).”

## Note

The source reports this as an across-agents task-type rate.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: result
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
CI tasks have a reported overall merge rate of 79%, placing them among the highest-success task types.

## Source Excerpt

“Across agents, tasks with consistently higher merge rates include documentations (84%), CI (79%), and build (74%).”

## Note

The source groups CI with other comparatively high-merge maintenance-oriented tasks.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Build tasks have a reported overall merge rate of 74% across agents.

## Source Excerpt

“Across agents, tasks with consistently higher merge rates include documentations (84%), CI (79%), and build (74%).”

## Note

This evidence is task-type specific and distinct from documentation and CI rates.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Performance tasks have the lowest reported overall merge rate across agents at 55%.

## Source Excerpt

“In contrast, performance (55%) and fix (64%) display the lowest merge rates overall.”

## Note

This evidence is limited to observed merge-rate differences by task type.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Fix tasks have a reported overall merge rate of 64%, placing them among the lowest-merge task types.

## Source Excerpt

“In contrast, performance (55%) and fix (64%) display the lowest merge rates overall.”

## Note

This evidence is separate from the lower performance-task merge rate.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Not-merged PRs show larger total lines-of-code changes than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta difference of 17%.

## Source Excerpt

“Based on Cliff’s δ (Table 1), the difference in total lines of code changes is 17%... both indicating a small-to-medium effect size that not-merged PRs skew toward larger changes.”

## Note

This is an association between PR size and merge outcome; it does not by itself establish why larger changes fail.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Not-merged PRs touch more files than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta difference of 10%.

## Source Excerpt

“Based on Cliff’s δ (Table 1), the difference in total lines of code changes is 17%, and the difference in the number of changed files is 10%... not-merged PRs skew toward larger changes.”

## Note

This is a reported distributional difference, not a direct causal test.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Not-merged PRs experience more CI failures than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta of 24%, described as a moderate effect size.

## Source Excerpt

“Figure 2(c) reports the distribution of CI failures for merged and not-merged agentic PRs... Cliff’s δ of 24% indicates a moderate effect size, showing that not-merged PRs tend to experience more CI failures.”

## Note

This evidence shows a stronger association than the size-related metrics, but still as an observed relationship.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: claim-support
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each additional failed CI check is associated with about a 15% decrease in the odds of a PR being merged.

## Source Excerpt

“Notably, each additional failed CI check decreases the odds of a merge by about 15%.”

## Note

This evidence comes from the reported logistic regression interpretation.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: method
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §2-§3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The qualitative rejection analysis began with 600 manually annotated rejected PRs, but 38 were inaccessible at analysis time, leaving 562 PRs for categorization.

## Source Excerpt

“We randomly select a subset of 600 rejected PRs for qualitative analysis...” and later, “We note that 38 PRs were no longer accessible at the time of analysis due to deletion or archival, leaving a total of 562 PRs for categorization.”

## Note

This evidence bounds the effective sample size used in the reported rejection-pattern distribution.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: incident
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Reviewer-level abandonment is the most frequent rejection pattern, affecting 228 PRs, or 38% of the manually analyzed rejected PRs.

## Source Excerpt

“Reviewer-level abandonment is the most frequent rejection pattern, accounting for 228 PRs (38%). These PRs were left without any meaningful human reviewer interaction...”

## Note

This rejection pattern reflects failure before substantive review, so it limits what can be inferred about code quality from rejection alone.
```

### file: evidence-17.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-17
kind: incident
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Duplicate PRs are the most common pull-request-level rejection pattern, affecting 142 PRs, or 23% of the annotated rejected PRs.

## Source Excerpt

“Within this level, duplicate PRs are the most common pattern, affecting 142 PRs (23%), where maintainers explicitly reference an existing PR that already implements the same change.”

## Note

This evidence identifies a specific rejection pattern within the PR-level category.
```

### file: evidence-18.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-18
kind: incident
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
CI/test failure is the most common code-level rejection pattern, affecting 99 PRs, or 17% of the annotated rejected PRs.

## Source Excerpt

“Code-level reasons represent the third most frequent category, affecting 133 PRs (22%). The dominant pattern in this category is CI/test failure, observed in 99 PRs (17%)...”

## Note

This evidence is specific to the code-level taxonomy rather than the full rejection distribution.
---

# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains empirical evidence about agent-authored GitHub pull request outcomes, including dataset scale, merge-rate differences by agent and task type, quantitative associations between non-merged PRs and change size / CI failure, and a qualitative taxonomy of rejection patterns from manually annotated failed PRs.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: result
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study dataset contains 33,596 agent-authored pull requests across five coding agents.

## Source Excerpt

“Of the 33,596 agentic pull requests, the majority originate from OpenAI Codex... Copilot and Devin follow... while Cursor contributes 1,541, and Claude Code represents the smallest share with 459 PRs.”

## Note

This is the reported dataset size for the analyzed PR corpus.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Across all agents, 24,014 of 33,596 PRs were merged, for a reported merge rate of 71.48%.

## Source Excerpt

“Across all agents, 71.48% of PRs (24,014) are successfully merged.”

## Note

This is the aggregate merge outcome across the full dataset.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
OpenAI Codex accounts for 21,799 PRs, the largest PR volume among the five agents in the dataset.

## Source Excerpt

“Of the 33,596 agentic pull requests, the majority originate from OpenAI Codex (21,799), exceeding the output of any other agent by more than a factor of four.”

## Note

This evidence is about contribution volume, not merge quality by itself.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
OpenAI Codex has the highest reported merge rate in the dataset at 82.59% (18,004 merged PRs).

## Source Excerpt

“Despite handling the largest volume of contributions, OpenAI Codex achieves the highest merge rate, with 82.59% (18,004) of its PRs being accepted.”

## Note

This evidence is descriptive of observed outcomes by agent.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-05

contradiction_relevance: none
---
Copilot has the lowest reported merge rate among the five agents at 43.04% (2,139 merged PRs).

## Source Excerpt

“Copilot exhibits the lowest merge rate, with only 43.04% (2,139) of its PRs being merged.”

## Note

This evidence compares observed agent-level merge outcomes.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Not-merged PRs show larger total lines-of-code changes than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta difference of 17%.

## Source Excerpt

“Based on Cliff’s δ (Table 1), the difference in total lines of code changes is 17%... both indicating a small-to-medium effect size that not-merged PRs skew toward larger changes.”

## Note

This is an association between PR size and merge outcome; it does not by itself establish why larger changes fail.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Not-merged PRs touch more files than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta difference of 10%.

## Source Excerpt

“Based on Cliff’s δ (Table 1), the difference in total lines of code changes is 17%, and the difference in the number of changed files is 10%... not-merged PRs skew toward larger changes.”

## Note

This is a reported distributional difference, not a direct causal test.
```

### file: evidence-13.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-13
kind: metric
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Not-merged PRs experience more CI failures than merged PRs, with a reported Cliff’s delta of 24%, described as a moderate effect size.

## Source Excerpt

“Figure 2(c) reports the distribution of CI failures for merged and not-merged agentic PRs... Cliff’s δ of 24% indicates a moderate effect size, showing that not-merged PRs tend to experience more CI failures.”

## Note

This evidence shows a stronger association than the size-related metrics, but still as an observed relationship.
```

### file: evidence-14.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-14
kind: claim-support
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 1
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Each additional failed CI check is associated with about a 15% decrease in the odds of a PR being merged.

## Source Excerpt

“Notably, each additional failed CI check decreases the odds of a merge by about 15%.”

## Note

This evidence comes from the reported logistic regression interpretation.
```

### file: evidence-15.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-15
kind: method
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §2-§3
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The qualitative rejection analysis began with 600 manually annotated rejected PRs, but 38 were inaccessible at analysis time, leaving 562 PRs for categorization.

## Source Excerpt

“We randomly select a subset of 600 rejected PRs for qualitative analysis...” and later, “We note that 38 PRs were no longer accessible at the time of analysis due to deletion or archival, leaving a total of 562 PRs for categorization.”

## Note

This evidence bounds the effective sample size used in the reported rejection-pattern distribution.
```

### file: evidence-16.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-16
kind: incident
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Reviewer-level abandonment is the most frequent rejection pattern, affecting 228 PRs, or 38% of the manually analyzed rejected PRs.

## Source Excerpt

“Reviewer-level abandonment is the most frequent rejection pattern, accounting for 228 PRs (38%). These PRs were left without any meaningful human reviewer interaction...”

## Note

This rejection pattern reflects failure before substantive review, so it limits what can be inferred about code quality from rejection alone.
```

### file: evidence-17.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-17
kind: incident
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Duplicate PRs are the most common pull-request-level rejection pattern, affecting 142 PRs, or 23% of the annotated rejected PRs.

## Source Excerpt

“Within this level, duplicate PRs are the most common pattern, affecting 142 PRs (23%), where maintainers explicitly reference an existing PR that already implements the same change.”

## Note

This evidence identifies a specific rejection pattern within the PR-level category.
```

### file: evidence-18.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-06-015
evidence_id: evidence-18
kind: incident
citation: Ehsani et al., arXiv 2601.15195v1, §3, Table 2
source_url: https://arxiv.org/html/2601.15195v1
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: none
---
CI/test failure is the most common code-level rejection pattern, affecting 99 PRs, or 17% of the annotated rejected PRs.

## Source Excerpt

“Code-level reasons represent the third most frequent category, affecting 133 PRs (22%). The dominant pattern in this category is CI/test failure, observed in 99 PRs (17%)...”

## Note

This evidence is specific to the code-level taxonomy rather than the full rejection distribution.
---
